body:
FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT COMMITTEE 
M I N U T E S 
 
Tuesday, March 17, 2026, 9:00 am 
Council Chamber, City Hall 
4949 Canada Way, Burnaby, BC 
 
PRESENT: 
Councillor Sav Dhaliwal, Chair 
Councillor Alison Gu, Vice Chair 
Mayor Mike Hurley, Member 
Councillor Pietro Calendino, Member 
Councillor Joe Keithley, Member (participated electronically) 
STAFF: 
Leon Gous, Chief Administrative Officer (CAO) 
Juli Halliwell, Acting Deputy CAO / General Manager Corporate Services / 
Corporate Officer 
Bob Klimek, Acting Chief Financial Officer 
Graham de la Gorgendiere, General Manager Community Safety 
May Phang, General Manager Engineering 
Kevan Cornes, Acting General Manager Lands & Facilities 
Mary Morrison-Clark, General Manager Parks, Recreation & Culture 
Ed Kozak, General Manager Planning & Development 
Sarah Alexander, Director Business Operations, Lands & Facilities 
Elaine Wong, Office Manager Mayor's Office (participated electronically) 
Denise Chak, Administrative Officer 1 
 
1. 
CALL TO ORDER 
The Chair, Councillor Dhaliwal, called the Committee meeting to order at 9:02 am. 
 
2. 
TERRITORIAL ACKNOWLEDGEMENT 
Chair Dhaliwal respectfully acknowledged that the City of Burnaby is located on the 
unceded territories of the xʷməθkʷəy̓ əm (Musqueam), Sḵwx̱ wú7mesh (Squamish), 
səlilwətaɬ (Tsleil-Waututh), and kʷikʷəƛ̓ əm (Kwikwetlem) Peoples. Each Nation has 
distinct histories and distinct traditional territories that fully or partially encompass the 
city. The City encourages you to learn more about the Host Nations whose ancestors 

Financial Management Committee Meeting 
Minutes March 17, 2026 
 
 
2 
have occupied and used these lands, including parts of present-day Burnaby, for 
thousands of years. 
 
3. 
ADOPTION OF AGENDA 
3.1 
Financial Management Committee Agenda for March 17, 2026 
MOVED AND SECONDED 
THAT the agenda for the Financial Management Committee meeting of March 
17, 2026, be adopted, AS AMENDED, to include a Notice of Motion under Other 
Business. 
CARRIED UNANIMOUSLY 
 
4. 
ADOPTION OF MINUTES 
4.1 
Minutes of the Financial Management Committee meeting held on February 
17, 2026 
MOVED AND SECONDED 
THAT the minutes of the Financial Management Committee meeting held on 
February 17, 2026, be now adopted. 
CARRIED UNANIMOUSLY 
 
5. 
DELEGATIONS AND INVITED PRESENTATIONS 
No delegation or presentation requests were received. 
 
6. 
ADMINISTRATIVE REPORTS 
No Administrative Reports were received. 
 
7. 
INFORMATION REPORTS 
7.1 
CITY INVESTMENTS - 2025 YEAR END REPORT 
The Deputy Chief Administrative Officer/Chief Financial Officer submitted a report 
providing Council with an update on the 2025 Investment Program and 
presenting a forecast for 2026. 
 
 

Financial Management Committee Meeting 
Minutes March 17, 2026 
 
 
3 
MOVED AND SECONDED 
THAT the report titled “City Investments – 2025 Year End Report”, dated March 
17, 2026, be received for information.  
CARRIED UNANIMOUSLY 
 
7.2 
SU: SIGNIFICANT CIVIC PROJECTS - MARCH 2026 
The General Manager Lands and Facilities submitted a report providing Council 
with a status update (SU) on significant civic projects. 
Mary Chow, Director Civic Projects, Isabelle Taillon, Recreation Expansion and 
Activation Lead, and Beth Davies, Chief Librarian, provided a PowerPoint 
presentation summarizing the report and highlighting the Cameron Community 
Centre and Library project.  
 
 
MOVED AND SECONDED 
THAT the report titled “SU: Significant Civic Projects – March 2026” dated March 
17, 2026, be received for information. 
CARRIED UNANIMOUSLY 
 
8. 
CORRESPONDENCE 
No items of correspondence were received. 
 
9. 
OTHER BUSINESS 
9.1 
NOTICE OF MOTION: Councillor Dhaliwal – External Review of City 
Financial Practices 
Chair Dhaliwal submitted a Notice of Motion seeking Council approval to engage 
an external reviewer to help safeguard the City’s assets and financial standing, 
and to mitigate the risk of significant tax increases and rising service costs for 
businesses and residents. Chair Dhaliwal noted that while the City’s financial 
position remains strong, sustaining the moderate tax increases of recent years is 
becoming increasingly challenging due to a sharp decline in density bonus 
revenues and significantly higher procurement costs. 
 
MOVED AND SECONDED 
THAT the Notice of Motion be waived. 
 
CARRIED UNANIMOUSLY 

Financial Management Committee Meeting 
Minutes March 17, 2026 
 
 
4 
With notice waived, the motion was put: 
 
MOVED AND SECONDED 
THAT engaging a qualified external reviewer to assess the City’s financial 
planning, operating practices, and capital investment priority setting, be 
approved; and  
THAT the reviewer liaise with the Financial Management Committee and deliver 
findings and recommendations directly to Council. 
CARRIED UNANIMOUSLY 
 
  
10. 
ADJOURNMENT 
By unanimous consent and without objection, the Committee meeting adjourned at 9:47 
am. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Councillor Sav Dhaliwal 
CHAIR 
 
Denise Chak 
ADMINISTRATIVE OFFICER

---

Purpose: To propose mid-year 2026 fee amendments to certain fee schedules within the Burnaby Consolidated Fees and Charges Bylaw.

Meeting April 21, 2026 
File: FM-13 
COMMITTEE REPORT 
 
TO: 
FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT COMMITTEE (FMC) 
 
FROM: 
DEPUTY CHIEF ADMINISTRATIVE OFFICER AND CHIEF 
FINANCIAL OFFICER  
 
 
SUBJECT: 
BURNABY CONSOLIDATED FEES AND CHARGES BYLAW - 2026 
MID-YEAR AMENDMENTS 
 
PURPOSE: 
To propose mid-year 2026 fee amendments to certain fee schedules 
within the Burnaby Consolidated Fees and Charges Bylaw. 
 
RECOMMENDATION 
THAT the proposed Parks, Recreation and Culture fee updates, as described in 
Sections 4.1 and 4.2, and as outlined in Attachments 1 and 2, of the report titled 
“Burnaby Consolidated Fees and Charges Bylaw – 2026 Mid-Year Amendments” 
dated April 21, 2026, be approved;  
THAT the City Solicitor be authorized to bring forward a bylaw to amend the 
Burnaby Consolidated Fees and Charges Bylaw, to amend Schedule F1 as 
described in Section 4.1 of the report, and substantially as set out in Attachment 1 
to the report, effective June 1, 2026; and 
THAT the City Solicitor be authorized to bring forward a bylaw to amend the 
Burnaby Consolidated Fees and Charges Bylaw, to repeal and replace 
Schedule F3, substantially as set out in Attachment 2 to the report, effective 
September 1, 2026. 
1.0 POLICY SECTION 
The Burnaby Consolidated Fees and Charges Bylaw (“Fees and Charges Bylaw”) 
provides the means by which most City of Burnaby fees and charges can be held under 
one document and managed in a timely and consistent manner. Fees and charges are 
imposed pursuant to Part 7, Division 2 – Fees, of the Community Charter, and section 462 
of the Local Government Act.\ 
 
 
 
 
 
 

Subject: Burnaby Consolidated Fees And Charges Bylaw - 2026 Mid Year Amendments  
Open Committee Name Meeting April 21, 2026 Page 2 
 
2.0 BACKGROUND 
Fees and charges are updated for all departments on an annual basis, however, due to 
the varied nature of municipal services and commencement date of key programs and 
initiatives, amendments are presented to Council at least three times per year. This report 
presents Parks, Recreation and Culture (PRC) fee amendments to Schedule F1 effective 
June 1, 2026, and Schedule F3 effective  September 1, 2026. Further cross departmental 
fee amendments will be presented in September 2026 for other  fees, effective January 
1, 2027, and then again in November 2026 for annual utility rates, also effective January 
1, 2027. Where necessary, additional amendments to the Fees and Charges Bylaw may 
be presented to Council at other time of the year.  
3.0 GENERAL INFORMATION 
3.1 Benchmark Rate Increase 
Staff are using a five-year average BC Headline rate for CPI to establish the 
benchmark general rate increase. The current benchmark general rate increase (as 
of March 13, 2026) for the mid-year update is 3.53%.  
3.2 Rounding 
To better reflect the benchmark rate increase, and to avoid sharp increases in user 
fees, the rounding rules for PRC are being revised. Going forward, all fees are now to 
be rounded up to the nearest 5 cents.  
4.0 KEY UPDATES 
4.1 Schedule F1 - Food Services 
To reflect the expanding nature of hospitality services provided by the City of Burnaby 
the following amendments are proposed for Schedule F1 to the Fees and Charges 
Bylaw, effective June 1, 2026:  
• Schedule F1 which is currently titled "Food Services" is proposed to be renamed 
"Burnaby Hospitality" 
 
• In addition to including fees and charges for Food Services, it is proposed that 
Schedule F1 include fees and charges for Hotel Burnaby, with fees and charges 
for room rates and related charges for Hotel Burnaby to be set as follows:  
Hotel Rates and Services 
Room rates and related charges will be determined in accordance with industry 
practice and be in line with market acceptance.  Room rates may include guest access 
to designated drop-in activities, such as gym and pool access, that would be available 
to the holder of a Be Active Pass. 
Food Services fees and charges will be updated in September for January 1, 2027.  

Subject: Burnaby Consolidated Fees And Charges Bylaw - 2026 Mid Year Amendments  
Open Committee Name Meeting April 21, 2026 Page 3 
 
4.2 Schedule F3 - Recreation and Culture 
It is proposed that Schedule F3 [Recreation and Culture] of the Fees and Charges 
Bylaw be repealed and replaced with a new Schedule F3, substantially in the form in 
Attachment 2, effective September 1, 2026.  The key changes to the fees and charges 
in Schedule F3 are summarized at the beginning of Attachment 2.  
5.0 COMMUNICATION AND COMMUNITY ENGAGEMENT 
Where necessary, impacted departments will inform their customers of the rate changes 
as outlined in this report. An updated consolidated version of the Fees and Charges Bylaw 
will be made available on the city website once adopted. 
 
6.0 FINANCIAL CONSIDERATIONS  
Finance will support Parks, Recreation and Culture in accounting for the proposed fee 
amendments as outlined in this report. While changes are not significant, effort is required 
in order to update the city's recreation system and related processes.  
 
Respectfully submitted, 
Richard Rowley, Director Finance - Revenue Services 
For 
Bob Klimek, Acting Chief Financial Officer 
ATTACHMENTS 
 Amendments to Schedule F1, Effective June 1, 2026 
Amendments to Schedule F3, Effective September 1, 2026 
REPORT CONTRIBUTORS 
This report was prepared by Richard Rowley, Director Finance – Revenue Services, and 
reviewed by: Judy Hamanishi – Director Recreation, Lyndsay Anderson – Manager, 
Recreation Services, and Jennifer Wong – Assistant City Solicitor.

Purpose: To provide a status update (SU) on significant civic projects. Staff presentation by Mary Chow, Director Civic Projects, and Isabelle Taillon, Recreation Expansion & Activation Lead

Meeting April 21, 2026 
File: ADM-18 
COMMITTEE REPORT 
 
TO: 
FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT COMMITTEE (FMC) 
 
FROM: 
GENERAL MANAGER LANDS AND FACILITIES  
 
 
SUBJECT: 
SU: SIGNIFICANT CIVIC PROJECTS – APRIL 2026 
 
PURPOSE: 
To provide a status update (SU) on significant civic projects. 
RECOMMENDATION 
THAT the report titled “SU: Significant Civic Projects – April 2026” dated April 21, 
2026, be received for information. 
EXECUTIVE SUMMARY 
This report provides a status update (SU) on significant civic projects managed by the 
Lands and Facilities Department (Civic Projects). 
1.0 POLICY SECTION 
Advancement of these projects is pursuant to the following major civic policies and 
strategies: 
 
• Official Community Plan (1998), 
• Social Sustainability Strategy (2011), 
• Environmental Sustainability Strategy (2016), 
• Burnaby Equity Policy (1994, updated 2020), 
• Burnaby Climate Action Framework (2020), and the 
• Burnaby Strategic Plan (2022). 
2.0 BACKGROUND 
The City of Burnaby has experienced significant growth and changing needs in recent 
years and is projected to grow by 100,000 new residents by 2050. In order to serve both 
existing and future residents, the City has identified the need for a number of significant 
civic projects in order to support its goals of creating vibrant communities and a city that 
is sustainable, inclusive, and livable. 
 
Civic projects typically progress through three primary development phases: planning, 
design, and construction, with some phases occurring concurrently depending on the 
project delivery method. Upon completion of the project, the new facility is handed over 
to the user department to establish operations of the programs and/or services. 
 
 

Subject: SU: Significant Civic Projects – April 2026 
Open FMC Meeting April 21, 2026 …………Page 2 
 
 
 
3.0 GENERAL INFORMATION 
A status update for the significant civic projects referenced in this report is provided 
below. Figure 1 is a visual overview of project progress referencing the primary 
development phases referenced in Section 2.0 above. Figure 2 provides an update on 
the spending against the overall budget for each project. 
 
 
Figure 1 – Project Schedules 
 
 
Figure 2 – Project Financials 
 
3.1 Mintara Restaurant Expansion 
The patio area at Mintara Restaurant atop Burnaby Mountain is being expanded into 
a 2,000 square-foot enclosed, all-weather patio. The renovation features glass 
windows, radiant floor heating and air conditioning, and a full bar and barbecue 
section. 
 
Concrete pours for the new foundations, foundation walls, and stairs have now been 
completed. Current and upcoming construction activities include the concrete pour 
for ground slab and installation of the ground floor glulam columns. The project 
remains on schedule and on budget. Project handover to the Parks, Recreation and 
Culture Department (PRC) is currently anticipated for and remains at Q3 2026. 
 

Subject: SU: Significant Civic Projects – April 2026 
Open FMC Meeting April 21, 2026 …………Page 3 
 
 
 
3.2 James Cowan Theatre Renewal 
As the new Shadbolt main stage, the James Cowan Theatre will be Burnaby’s 
primary professional multi-use presentation space. The new facility will cater to a 
variety of arts programming, including theatre, visual arts, literary and media arts, 
and events. Construction is progressing with exterior cladding, interior framing, 
drywalling, and elevator installation work underway. Construction impacts continue 
to be mitigated as much as possible through measures such as in-place parking 
restrictions and maintaining pedestrian access. Project handover to PRC is currently 
anticipated for Q1 2027. The project remains on schedule and on budget. 
 
3.3 Burnaby Lake Recreation Centre 
The Burnaby Lake Recreation Centre will be one of the largest aquatic facilities in 
BC and a regional hub for recreation and sport, featuring a ten-lane 50-metre pool, a 
combined leisure and six-lane 25-metre lap-pool, and an NHL-sized arena. A range 
of community spaces are also incorporated in the design, including multipurpose 
rooms and the Burnaby Sports Hall of Fame. Construction milestones include the 
structural steel erection of the administrative lobby area that has recently been 
completed and waterproofing of the 50-metre pool basin that is currently in progress. 
 
Preliminary design concepts and technical analysis to assess a potential pedestrian 
overpass, examining a range of options, feasibility considerations, and cost recovery 
approaches are now complete. The information gathered will help inform future 
planning decisions and broader priorities. 
 
Roadworks on Joe Sakic Way, Sperling Avenue, and Sprott Street for new sidewalks 
and wider pavement will continue with some traffic impacts until the fall of this year. 
Public parking and pedestrian access to Burnaby Lake East continue to be 
maintained. Additional geotechnical work to support the road improvements is 
required to mitigate the existing challenging soil conditions. 
 
Project handover to PRC is currently anticipated for Q1 2028 with the project 
currently trending ahead of schedule. The project remains on budget. A project 
highlights presentation is scheduled for the Committee at its April 2026 meeting. 
 
3.4 Community Safety Building 
A new three-story, 129,000-square-foot RCMP detachment is being constructed at 
the corner of Ledger Avenue and Norland Avenue to replace the existing 
detachment. Current construction activities include concrete pours and exterior 
enclosure construction. 
 
Design of the project is well underway and progressing in accordance with target 
value design principles to maximize value and proactive design solutions. The 
design processes have been completed for the interior walls, ceilings, and exterior 

Subject: SU: Significant Civic Projects – April 2026 
Open FMC Meeting April 21, 2026 …………Page 4 
 
 
 
design of the building envelope. Design work is continuing this month for the interior 
requirements and site/landscaping plans, with design completion anticipated for Q1 
2027 and design progress currently trending ahead of schedule. 
 
Project handover is anticipated for the end of Q4 2027, with the RCMP 
commissioning period anticipated to be approximately six months. The project 
remains on schedule and on budget. 
3.5 Cameron Community Centre and Library 
The new Cameron Community Centre and Library will be the largest community 
centre in Burnaby, featuring an aquatic facility, gymnasium, weight room, expanded 
library, and civic plaza. 
 
Construction continues with the demolition, excavation, and substructure now fully 
completed, and work is currently underway for construction of the structural steel, 
mass timber, and the mechanical and electrical rough-ins. Project handover to PRC 
is currently anticipated for Q2 2028. The project remains on budget. 
 
3.6 Brentwood Community Centre 
The Brentwood Community Centre will be a multi-level facility within the Brentwood 
Block development site, featuring community gathering spaces, a fitness centre, a 
gymnasium, a podium-level outdoor park, music and art rooms, and a public plaza. 
The project schedule aligns with the Grosvenor Brentwood Master Plan 
development timelines and development of the operating agreement is underway. 
Construction on the development site is currently underway, with construction of the 
community centre portions of the building currently anticipated to start in Q3 2027 
and be completed in Q1 2030. The project remains on budget. 
3.7 Community Facilities Fit-Out Project 
The Community Facilities Fit-Out Project will provide the required furniture, fixtures, 
and equipment for the James Cowan Theatre, the Community Safety Building, 
Burnaby Lake Recreation Centre, Cameron Community Centre and Library, and the 
Brentwood Community Centre. This project will provide a streamlined approach to 
support the fitting out of these major capital projects and to maximize efficiencies 
through economies of scale. 
 
Delivery and installation of the required furniture, fixtures, and equipment will be 
coordinated with each individual project timeline to ensure all fit-out requirements will 
be in place for the opening of each facility. The coordination work as part of this 
project spans both capital and operating needs and the required expenditures will 
need to be funded accordingly through the annual budget process. The initial 
procurement processes are currently underway to deliver on the fitting out of the 
James Cowan Theatre project, along with design coordination underway for the 

Subject: SU: Significant Civic Projects – April 2026 
Open FMC Meeting April 21, 2026 …………Page 5 
 
 
 
other major projects. The coordination work will continue until the completion of the 
last major project in 2030. 
4.0 COMMUNICATION AND COMMUNITY ENGAGEMENT 
Communication plans are in place for each project, with ongoing engagement with key 
users and staff groups. External communication to the wider public regarding any 
impacts occurs through dedicated project webpages, public notices via the City’s 
communication channels, and continued outreach by City staff. 
5.0 FINANCIAL CONSIDERATIONS 
Funding for the noted significant civic building projects is included in the 2026-2030 
Capital Plan. Budget requirements for operations will be brought forward by the Parks, 
Recreation and Culture Department for Council’s consideration as the subject projects 
advance. 
Respectfully submitted, 
 
James Lota, General Manager Lands and Facilities 
REPORT CONTRIBUTORS 
This report was prepared by Janelle Frank, Senior Manager Civic Projects and 
reviewed by David Rose, Manager Recreation Services; Mary Morrison-Clark, 
General Manager Parks Recreation & Culture; and Mary Chow, Director Civic 
Projects.

Purpose: To provide a SU on the IT initiatives for January to March 2026.

Meeting April 21, 2026 
File: 2410-20 / 3055-20 
COMMITTEE REPORT 
 
TO: 
FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT COMMITTEE (FMC) 
 
FROM: 
CHIEF INFORMATION OFFICER  
 
 
SUBJECT: 
SU: IT INITIATIVES - JANUARY TO MARCH 2026 
 
PURPOSE: 
To provide a status update (SU) on the IT initiatives for January to March 
2026. 
RECOMMENDATION 
THAT the report titled "SU: IT Initiatives - January to March 2026" dated April 21, 
2026, be received for information. 
EXECUTIVE SUMMARY 
The purpose of this report is to update Committee and Council on the status of 2026 
Information Technology (IT) initiatives delivered by IT in collaboration with City 
departments and provide a look ahead at their upcoming work plan and schedule. 
1.0 POLICY SECTION 
Not Applicable. 
2.0 BACKGROUND 
Since March 2023, IT has been reporting to the Financial Management Committee on 
major IT initiatives on a quarterly basis. The following information is an update on IT 
initiatives for January to March 2026. 
3.0 GENERAL INFORMATION 
IT Initiatives Progress Report 
This year, we have introduced an enhanced format for reporting on major IT initiatives. 
In addition to capital projects reported in previous years, this updated report now 
includes both capital and operating initiatives, providing Council with a more 
comprehensive view of progress in executing the City’s Digital Strategy. The report also 
includes key milestone updates for each initiative. We welcome Council’s feedback on 
this new format. 
Please refer to Attachment 1 for the IT Initiatives Progress Report. 
Major IT projects progress through the following four project phases:  

Subject: SU: IT Initiatives– January to March 2026 
Open FMC Meeting April 21, 2026 Page 2 
 
1. Initiation: Define the project’s purpose, objectives, department and resident 
stakeholders, high-level scope, costs, options analysis and feasibility to formally 
authorize the project and align it with digital strategic goals. 
2. Planning: Develop technical and business requirements, tender or Request for 
Proposal (RFP) evaluation, vendor selection, and contract negotiation. Establish the 
detailed roadmap for delivery, including scope, schedule, resources, budget, risks, and 
governance, with appropriate tailoring for agile, waterfall, or hybrid approaches. 
3. Execution: Deliver the project outcomes by coordinating people and resources, 
managing work, and adapting to changes while ensuring quality and stakeholder 
engagement. Develop, test, configure, and release the project to production.   
4. Close: Formally complete the project or phase by obtaining acceptance, transitioning 
deliverables, documenting lessons learned, and releasing resources. Supports project 
launch, monitoring and control and closure, including release activities, bug fixes and 
minor improvements.  
Upon completion of the project, the hardware, software, system and processes are 
operationalized with the customer departments, and a plan is prepared for regular 
sustainment activities to be led by IT team.  
The following is a visual representation of schedules of significant IT projects identifying 
the phases outlined in this section. 
 
4.0 COMMUNICATION AND COMMUNITY ENGAGEMENT 
For all projects that interface with citizens, IT works closely with the Marketing & 
Communications Department on communication plans to citizens to increase their 
awareness of these new online services and to gather feedback where required. 
5.0 FINANCIAL CONSIDERATIONS  
Funding for these IT initiatives are included in the 2026-2030 Capital Plan and 
Operating Plan.  

Subject: SU: IT Initiatives– January to March 2026 
Open FMC Meeting April 21, 2026 Page 3 
 
Respectfully submitted, 
Bachar Khawajah, Chief Information Officer 
 
ATTACHMENTS 
Attachment 1 - Key IT Initiatives January-March 2026 Status Update 
 
REPORT CONTRIBUTORS 
This report was prepared by the IT team and reviewed by Swapna Tembhekar, Acting 
Director IT Planning and Governance, Jesse Hausner, Director Enterprise Systems and 
Bhavan Mahal, Acting Director IT Infrastructure and Operations.

Purpose: To provide a status update (SU) on select Engineering Projects.

Meeting April 21, 2026 
File: ADM-18 
COMMITTEE REPORT 
 
TO: 
FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT COMMITTEE (FMC) 
 
FROM: 
GENERAL MANAGER ENGINEERING  
 
 
SUBJECT: 
SU: MAJOR ENGINEERING PROJECTS – DECEMBER 2025 - 
APRIL 2026 
 
PURPOSE: 
To provide a status update (SU) on select Engineering Projects. 
RECOMMENDATION 
THAT the report titled “SU: Major Engineering Projects – December 2025 - April 
2026” dated April 21, 2026, be received for information. 
1.0 POLICY SECTION 
The advancement of these projects aligns with the community pillar of the Burnaby 
Strategic Plan and is further supported by the Burnaby Transportation Plan and various 
Asset Management Plans. 
2.0 BACKGROUND 
The Engineering Department has identified the following projects for regular Committee 
updates. These projects were selected to represent a broad cross section of the 
engineering annual work plan and correspond with public interest. 
1. Marine Drive: Greenall Avenue to Strathearn Avenue 
2. Sidewalk Expansion Program - Alpha Secondary School 
3. Burnaby Lake Pedestrian Bridge Overpass 
4. Vancouver-SFU Active Transportation Connection (Phase 2) Boundary to Duthie 
5. Sidewalk Upgrades – Carleton, Madison, and Triumph 
Throughout a project lifecycle, engineering projects progress through several phases.  
In the interest of reporting, these phases can be summarized into design and 
construction. 
1. Design: In this phase, the design is developed to a level of detail suitable for 
construction. General tasks include preliminary design, environmental and regulatory 
permitting, interested parties’ engagement, detailed design, and construction 
procurement. 
2. Construction: In this phase, the plans developed in the design phase are carried out 
by the successful contractor. Throughout construction, the City monitors construction 
progress and liaises with interested parties. 

Subject: SU: MAJOR ENGINEERING PROJECTS - DECEMBER 2025 - APRIL 2026 
Open FMC Meeting April 21, 2026 Page 2 
 
Upon completion of a successful project, the new assets are handed over to the 
Infrastructure and Public Works divisions to operate and administer the programs and/or 
services. 
3.0 GENERAL INFORMATION 
The following is a visual representation of the schedules of major Engineering projects 
identifying the development phases outlined in Section 2.0:  
      
 
3.1 Marine Drive: Greenall Avenue to Strathearn Avenue 
Background and scope: This project will bring Marine Drive from Greenall Avenue to 
Strathearn Avenue to the urban street standard, including curb and gutter (lane 
widths of 3.9m), sidewalk on both sides of the road, bus route pavement, new bus 
stops, intersection upgrades, new streetlights, boulevard, and street trees as 
required. 
Solid Waste & Recycling (SWR) collection will remain on Marine Drive due to the 
limited lane access for collection vehicles.  

Subject: SU: MAJOR ENGINEERING PROJECTS - DECEMBER 2025 - APRIL 2026 
Open FMC Meeting April 21, 2026 Page 3 
 
Schedule: The utilities construction has been completed. Roadworks construction is 
anticipated to begin 2028/2029 pending funding availability. Overall, the project 
remains on schedule. 
Total estimated value including design and overhead: $13,100,000. 
Spent to date: $3,826,000. 
Percentage completion to date: 33%   
 
 
3.2 Sidewalk Expansion Program - Alpha Secondary School 
Background and scope: Project scope includes new sidewalks, curb and gutter, front 
boulevard with street trees, utility upgrades, IT communication conduit, and 
streetlight upgrades. 
Schedule: This project has achieved Substantial Completion in early February. 
Contractor is working on deficiencies  
Total estimated value including design and overhead: $10,500,000. 
Spent to date: $9,711,000. 
Percentage completion to date: 95% 

Subject: SU: MAJOR ENGINEERING PROJECTS - DECEMBER 2025 - APRIL 2026 
Open FMC Meeting April 21, 2026 Page 4 
 
 
 

Subject: SU: MAJOR ENGINEERING PROJECTS - DECEMBER 2025 - APRIL 2026 
Open FMC Meeting April 21, 2026 Page 5 
 
3.3 Burnaby Lake Pedestrian Bridge Overpass  
Background and scope: The pedestrian-cyclist overpass will support multiple regional 
initiatives and strengthen the broader regional active transportation network. This 
includes improving connectivity with Metro Vancouver’s Central Valley Greenway 
system and TransLink’s Major Bikeway Network. This project was identified as part of 
the City’s Transportation Plan.  
Schedule: 100% civil, architectural, structural, electrical and landscaping design is 
complete ($3m). Construction activities on the north and south abutments are 
complete. Final bridge installation over Highway 1 was completed in early November 
2025. North / south plazas are in the final phase of construction, and the project is on 
schedule to achieve total completion by Q2 2026. Due to a contract requirement for 
the owner to pay for welding inspection services and certain design changes to the 
bridge electrical systems, additional funds were approved as part of the change 
management system. Final costs are expected to be approximately $27,800,000 
inclusive of contract administration, final inspections, grand opening events and minor 
safety related change orders.   
 
Total estimated value including design and overhead: $27,000,000. 
Spent to date: $27,540,000. 
Percentage completion to date: 100% 
 

Subject: SU: MAJOR ENGINEERING PROJECTS - DECEMBER 2025 - APRIL 2026 
Open FMC Meeting April 21, 2026 Page 6 
 
 
3.4 Vancouver-SFU Active Transportation Connection (Phase 2) Boundary to 
Duthie 
Background and scope: The Vancouver SFU Cycling Connection project directly 
supports the City’s goals in developing a cycling network that is attractive, 
comfortable, and safe for cyclists of All Ages and Abilities (AAA). This project aims to 
connect the existing Adanac Bikeway in Vancouver with Simon Fraser University 
(SFU) via the Frances-Union Bikeway (FUB) and the Burnaby Mountain Parkway 
(BMP). This project also aligns with TransLink’s Transport 2050 Major Bikeway 
Network and is partially funded by Infrastructure Canada’s Active Transportation 
Fund. 
Phase 2 will consist of installing raised intersections and continuous sidewalks on 
the FUB, and a new multi-use pathway (MUP) adjacent to Kensington Park. This 
work is currently in final detailed design and is anticipated to be tendered in Q1 
2026.  
Schedule: Phase 2 design is nearing completion, and construction will be tendered 
in early 2026. Phase 2 works will be on Union Street (Boundary Road to Ingleton 
Avenue), Ingleton Avenue (Union Street to Frances Street), Frances Street (Ingleton 
Avenue to Holdom Avenue), Hammarskjold Drive (dead end to Kensington Avenue), 
and Union Street (Kensington Avenue to Duthie Avenue).  
Total estimated value including design and overhead: $4,000,000 (Phase 2) 
Spent to date: $107,000. 
Percentage completion to date: 25% (Phase 2) 
 

Subject: SU: MAJOR ENGINEERING PROJECTS - DECEMBER 2025 - APRIL 2026 
Open FMC Meeting April 21, 2026 Page 7

Source: https://yourvoice.burnaby.ca/vancouver-sfu-cycling-connection 
 
3.5 Sidewalk Upgrades – Carleton, Madison, and Triumph 
Background and scope: Project scope includes new sidewalks, curb and gutter, front 
boulevard with street trees, utility upgrades, IT communication conduit, and 
streetlight upgrades. 
Schedule: The project design phase started in 2024 and an open house including 
additional scope was completed on Oct 29, 2025, and was well received by the 
residents. Based on the comments / suggestions received, the design was finalized 
and tender has been posted out for bidding and closing on Mar 24, 2026. The 
construction is expected to start in Q2 2026 and will last till Q2 of 2027. All the Hydro 
and Telus relocations are underway to prevent project delays.  
Total estimated value including design and overhead: $16,800,000. 
Spent to date: $555,000. 
Percentage completion to date: 3%

Project information is also available on the City of Burnaby website.  
5.0 FINANCIAL CONSIDERATIONS  
Funding for these major Engineering projects is included in the 2026-2030 Capital Plan 
and servicing agreements with developers. 
 
Respectfully submitted, 
 
May Phang, P. Eng., General Manager Engineering

briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Recreation, Hotel, and Hospitality Fee Changes Up for Approval
briefs.summary: Burnaby's Financial Management Committee is set to vote on mid-year changes to the city's official fee schedule (a bylaw — a local law — that sets what people and businesses pay to use city services). A proposed bylaw would rename the Food Services schedule to 'Burnaby Hospitality,' add room rates for a new Hotel Burnaby, and update dozens of recreation fees — including passes, arena ice time, pool lanes, and facility rentals — with most rising roughly 3.5% in line with inflation. If approved, the hospitality and food service changes would take effect June 1, 2026, and the recreation and culture fee changes would take effect September 1, 2026.
briefs.action_required: Review the proposed new fee schedules now — check if your business rents city facilities or operates food concessions.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Consolidated Fees and Charges Bylaw — Schedule F1 (Burnaby Hospitality) amendments take effect
Burnaby Consolidated Fees and Charges Bylaw — Schedule F3 (Recreation and Culture) fee changes take effect

briefs.industries: Hotel & Accommodation, Food Service, Recreation & Leisure

briefs.title: Council to Consider Hiring External Reviewer to Assess Burnaby's Financial Practices
briefs.summary: Burnaby's Financial Management Committee has approved a motion — now heading to full Council for a final vote — to hire an outside expert to review how the City plans its budget, runs its operations, and prioritizes major spending. The move comes as density bonus revenues (fees developers pay for extra building height or density) have dropped sharply and procurement costs have risen, raising concerns about future tax increases for businesses and residents.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the full Council vote — findings could influence future tax rates and city service costs.
briefs.industries: Finance & Insurance, Construction & Development, Professional Services

briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby to update Parks, Recreation, and Hotel Burnaby fees mid-2026
briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on mid-year fee changes to Burnaby's Fees and Charges Bylaw. A proposed bylaw would rename the Food Services fee schedule to 'Burnaby Hospitality' and add room rates for Hotel Burnaby (effective June 1, 2026), while Recreation and Culture fees would be updated under a new schedule (effective September 1, 2026), with most rates rising roughly 3.53% based on a five-year average inflation measure.
briefs.action_required: Check the updated fee schedules on the Burnaby city website once adopted — new rates take effect June 1 and September 1, 2026.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Hospitality (Hotel Burnaby) fee schedule takes effect
Burnaby Recreation and Culture fee schedule (Schedule F3) takes effect

briefs.title: Council to Consider: Status Update on Seven Major Burnaby Civic Construction Projects
briefs.summary: Burnaby's Financial Management Committee is set to receive a status update on seven major city construction projects, including a new recreation centre at Burnaby Lake, the Cameron Community Centre and Library, a new RCMP building, and more. No new approvals are being sought — this is an information update only, giving councillors and the public a progress report on projects already underway or in planning.
briefs.action_required: Monitor road and traffic impacts near Burnaby Lake (Joe Sakic Way, Sperling Ave, Sprott St) through fall 2026.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Lake road works traffic impacts end — Joe Sakic Way, Sperling Ave, Sprott St

briefs.title: Council to consider: Burnaby IT department's Q1 2026 project status update
briefs.summary: Burnaby's Financial Management Committee is set to receive a status update on the City's major Information Technology (IT) projects from January to March 2026. The report covers both capital projects (large, one-time investments) and ongoing operating initiatives, giving Council a broader picture of how the City's Digital Strategy is being carried out.

briefs.title: Council to Consider: Status Update on Five Major Burnaby Engineering Projects
briefs.summary: Burnaby's Financial Management Committee is set to receive a status update on five major city engineering projects at its April 21, 2026 meeting. The projects include road upgrades on Marine Drive, new sidewalks near Alpha Secondary School, the nearly complete Burnaby Lake Pedestrian Bridge, a new cycling connection toward SFU, and sidewalk upgrades on Carleton, Madison, and Triumph streets — with combined estimated costs exceeding $71 million.
briefs.action_required: Check if any project runs near your business and plan for possible road or access disruptions.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Lake Pedestrian Bridge — projected total completion
Marine Drive roadworks construction — anticipated start
